ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for hot chocolate premix with marshmallows; closest defensible match is hot cocoa drink mix (FOODON_03301099). Marshmallow component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers cocoa-based beverage powders; marshmallow inclusion is not separately represented in FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hot chocolate premix with marshmallows as a food substance.

preparation_method: reconstituted with hot water or milk

serving.description: 1 sachet / 1 prepared cup (approximately 28 g premix powder)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 28 g of premix powder (one sachet), typically dissolved in 180–200 mL of hot water or milk to yield one cup of hot chocolate.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Chocolate with Marshmallows (Premix) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hot chocolate with marshmallows (premix), cr_id D04020013. Values reported per 100 g of premix powder. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient detail not reported in HPB entry; set to -1 where unavailable. Calcium and iron reported as 0.0 in HPB source — confirmed zero per HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cocoa-Based Instant Drink Mixes — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for this specific branded premix was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on cocoa-based instant drink mixes and hot chocolate powder entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 35–45 for similar sweetened cocoa powder beverages. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 10.92 × 0.28) / 100 ≈ 1.2 per 28 g serving; the input record's stated GL of 4.4 appears to reflect a larger reconstituted serving basis (approximately 100 g prepared beverage). GL of 4.4 retained from HPB input record as it aligns with a ~100 g prepared serving reference. Confidence in GI estimate is moderate-low (0.34) given absence of direct measurement.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.21 g/100 g) in a sweetened instant drink mix; low potassium and zero calcium further limit cardiovascular benefit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweetened instant hot chocolate premixes, as the high sugar load (8.21 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, and the absence of meaningful potassium or calcium provides no offsetting cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (8.21 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (0.53 g/100 g), and marshmallow inclusions (corn syrup, sugar) elevating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sweetened premix beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high free sugar content (8.21 g/100 g), minimal fibre (0.53 g/100 g), and the presence of marshmallows made from corn syrup and sugar, which collectively produce a rapid glycaemic response; it should be avoided entirely in diabetes management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (8.21 g/100 g), ultra-processed formulation with potential palm oil or hydrogenated fat, and zero calcium and iron
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this ultra-processed sweetened cocoa premix, as its high sugar content (8.21 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and metabolic risk, and the formulation may contain palm oil or partially hydrogenated fats that adversely affect LDL cholesterol; the absence of calcium and iron provides no cardioprotective nutritional value.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar (8.21 g/100 g) and corn syrup from marshmallows promote pro-inflammatory mediators; zero calcium is a concern for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this premix, as its high refined sugar content (8.21 g/100 g) and corn syrup from marshmallows promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, and the absence of calcium is particularly concerning for RA patients on corticosteroid therapy who require bone protection.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Chocolate with Marshmallows (Premix) is an ultra-processed instant powdered beverage mix combining cocoa powder, sugar, maltodextrin, and mini marshmallows (made from corn syrup, sugar, and gelatin). It is reconstituted by dissolving one sachet (approximately 28 g) in 180–200 mL of hot water or milk to produce a sweet, creamy, chocolate-flavoured hot drink. The resulting beverage has a mild cocoa flavour with a sweet, slightly marshmallow-forward taste and a smooth, thin texture. The premix is commercially produced and widely available in supermarkets across Singapore and internationally. Per 100 g of premix powder, it provides 54 kcal, 0.54 g protein, 0.9 g fat, 10.92 g carbohydrate (of which 8.21 g is free sugar), 0.53 g dietary fibre, and 54 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as zero in the HPB source. The high free sugar content (8.21 g/100 g) and ultra-processed nature of this product make it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The marshmallow component adds corn syrup and gelatin, further elevating the glycaemic and inflammatory burden. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low GI range) based on analogous cocoa-based instant drink mixes in the University of Sydney GI Database, though the high free sugar content means glycaemic load remains a clinical concern. This product is not a meaningful source of micronutrients and should be considered an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary component.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink hot chocolate with marshmallows?
How many calories in hot chocolate premix?
Is instant hot chocolate high in sugar?
Is hot chocolate with marshmallows suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of hot chocolate premix?
Can I drink hot chocolate if I have cardiovascular disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04020013), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with moderate-low confidence (0.34) due to absence of a direct measurement for this specific product; overall record confidence is rated high based on the strength of the HPB nutrient data.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral micronutrient fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and micronutrient data for this hot chocolate premix with marshmallows are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (cr_id D04020013) as of 2026. 2. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database source. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on cocoa-based instant drink mixes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was identified. 4. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input record are noted but not carried forward as formal dietary flags, as the high free sugar content (8.21 g/100 g) makes 'low_calorie' characterisation clinically misleading for this product. 5. Halal status is unconfirmed; gelatin in marshmallows may be pork-derived, making halal certification uncertain without product-level verification. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for hot chocolate premix with marshmallows as a combined product.
